ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302397 covers naan bread generically. This record represents a mixed basket of four naan varieties (plain, butter, garlic, cheese); no single exact FoodOn term exists for the composite basket.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers leavened flatbreads broadly. The composite naan basket with butter, garlic, and cheese toppings is not individually coded; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226559009 covers naan bread as a substance. The composite basket presentation is not individually coded; closest_parent applied.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical naan basket served at an Indian restaurant contains four naan pieces — one each of plain, butter, garlic, and cheese — weighing approximately 400 g in total, suitable for sharing among two to three diners.

nutrient_source.title: Naan bread, plain / butter / garlic / cheese — composite estimate based on ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for naan and enriched flatbread
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a mixed naan basket. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted average of four naan types: plain naan (ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, maida-based leavened bread), butter naan (plain naan + ~5 g butter per 100 g), garlic naan (plain naan + garlic paste, negligible macro change), and cheese naan (plain naan + ~10 g processed cheese per 100 g). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Bread, naan, plain, commercially prepared, refrigerated' (FDC ID 174924) used as cross-reference. The composite per-100 g values represent an approximate equal-weight blend of the four varieties.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Naan bread, white wheat flour, baked in tandoor oven
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 71 is drawn from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white wheat flour naan baked in a tandoor oven (GI ~71, high). The composite basket blends plain, butter, garlic, and cheese naan; butter and cheese additions modestly reduce GI but the dominant carbohydrate matrix remains refined maida, so GI 71 is retained as the best defensible estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (71 × 190 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 ≈ 135.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1920 mg per basket serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit naan basket consumption to one piece per meal and avoid additional high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salted chutneys, as a full basket can contribute approximately 80–96% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 71) and high refined carbohydrate load from maida-based naan
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict naan basket intake to one small piece per meal, pair it with high-fibre vegetables or legume-based curries to blunt postprandial glucose excursions, and monitor blood glucose response given the high glycemic load of approximately 135 per full basket serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter and cheese (~3.9 g per 100 g; ~15.6 g per basket) and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit naan basket frequency to occasional consumption, preferring plain naan over butter or cheese varieties to reduce saturated fat intake, and should account for the combined sodium and saturated fat burden when planning daily dietary targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A Naan Basket (Plain, Butter, Garlic and Cheese) is a shared Indian restaurant offering consisting of four varieties of tandoor-baked leavened flatbread served together in a single basket. Each naan is made from maida (refined white wheat flour) leavened with yeast or yogurt and baked at high heat in a clay tandoor oven, producing a soft, slightly charred, pillowy bread with a chewy interior and lightly crisp exterior. The plain naan is the base form; the butter naan is brushed generously with melted butter after baking; the garlic naan is topped with minced garlic and fresh coriander; and the cheese naan is stuffed or topped with paneer or processed cheese. The basket format is popular in Indian restaurants across Singapore, including North Indian and Mughlai establishments, and is typically ordered as a shared side to accompany curries, dals, and tandoori dishes. Nutritionally, the basket is energy-dense (~298 kcal per 100 g) with a high refined carbohydrate content (~47.5 g per 100 g) and a high glycemic index of approximately 71, making it a high-glycemic food. The butter and cheese varieties contribute meaningful saturated fat (~3.9 g per 100 g) and the sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g) is clinically significant. The basket is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains gluten and dairy allergens. Clinically, it is a high-concern food for Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI and glycemic load, and a moderate-concern food for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and saturated fat content. It is best consumed in moderation and paired with fibre-rich accompaniments to moderate glycemic impact.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a naan basket?
Is naan basket suitable for diabetics?
What types of naan are in a naan basket?
What is the GI of naan bread?
How much sodium is in naan?
Can I eat naan if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between plain, butter, garlic and cheese naan?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a composite naan basket. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted average of four naan varieties using ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as cross-references. GI is drawn from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain white wheat flour naan (GI 71) and applied as an analogous estimate for the composite basket. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and grounded in approved sources, but the composite nature of the dish introduces uncertainty.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent an approximate equal-weight composite of plain, butter, garlic, and cheese naan based on ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy data. Actual values will vary by restaurant preparation, portion size, and the ratio of naan types in the basket. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy source entries for naan bread. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for naan bread; USDA SR Legacy reports a value for commercial naan but the composite basket estimate does not have a defensible blended selenium figure from the primary source. 4. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI 71 × 190 g available carbohydrate in a 400 g basket) / 100 = 134.9, rounded to 135.0. 5. The food_group has been corrected from the input value 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Grains and staples' as naan is a bread product; dairy is a secondary ingredient. 6. The basket is served at Anjana Kitchen – Six Battery Road (field survey data source). 7. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule does not apply.
